questions.question: You send a UDP packet with ARQ — the packet arrives, but your ACK gets lost. What happens next without sequence numbers?
options: The sender knows the ACK was lost because UDP tracks delivery
The sender retransmits the packet, and the receiver delivers it to the application a second time — a duplicate
The receiver detects the missing ACK and resends it automatically
Nothing — the sender assumes success after a timeout
questions.explanation: Without sequence numbers, the receiver cannot distinguish a retransmission from new data. When the sender times out and retransmits, the receiver sees an identical packet — it has no way to know it already delivered this data. It delivers the payload again, causing application-level data corruption. Sequence numbers solve this: the receiver remembers the last sequence it accepted and discards packets with the same number.

questions.question: Stop-and-wait ARQ has a sender utilisation of about 1% on a 100ms RTT, 100 Mbps link with 1500-byte packets. Why?
options: Stop-and-wait uses 99% of bandwidth for headers and control traffic
The sender is idle for most of the RTT, waiting for each ACK before it can send the next packet
The protocol limits transmission to 1% of link speed by design
1500-byte packets are too small for 100 Mbps links
questions.explanation: Throughput = packet_size / (RTT + transmission_time). For 1500 bytes over 100 Mbps: transmission_time = 0.12ms. RTT = 100ms. Time per packet ≈ 100.12ms. Throughput = 1500 / 0.10012 ≈ 14,980 bytes/s ≈ 120 Kbps. Utilisation = 120 Kbps / 100 Mbps ≈ 0.12%. The sender spends 99.88% of each cycle waiting. The sliding window (pipelining) protocol fixes this by allowing multiple packets in flight simultaneously.

questions.question: In the alternating-bit protocol, the sender sends seq=0, the receiver ACKs seq=0, but the ACK is lost. The sender retransmits seq=0. What does the receiver do?
options: It delivers the retransmitted data to the application again
It discards the duplicate (seq=0 was already accepted) and re-sends ACK seq=0
It sends a NACK (negative acknowledgment) telling the sender to start over
It waits for the original ACK to arrive before responding to the retransmit
questions.explanation: The receiver accepted seq=0 and advanced its expected sequence to 1. When it sees another packet with seq=0, it knows this is a retransmit of something already delivered. It discards the payload (to avoid duplicates) but still sends ACK seq=0 — this un-blocks the sender. Without this re-ACK, the sender would timeout and retransmit forever, never progressing to seq=1.

questions.question: In our ARQ implementation, why does the sender call `sock.settimeout(TIMEOUT)` instead of using a separate timer thread?
options: Thread-based timers are less accurate than socket timeouts
settimeout makes recv() raise socket.timeout after the specified time, which naturally triggers the retransmit logic in the same code path
Python does not support timer threads in network programs
Socket timeouts automatically retransmit the packet; a thread timer would not
questions.explanation: Setting a socket timeout makes blocking calls like recv() raise socket.timeout after the specified duration. This is clean and simple: the sender calls recv() to wait for an ACK, and if it doesn't arrive within TIMEOUT seconds, the exception is caught and we retransmit. A separate timer thread would add complexity (coordination, shared state, thread safety). The socket timeout approach keeps all the ARQ logic in a single sequential loop.

questions.question: What is the maximum number of distinct in-flight packets that stop-and-wait ARQ can support?
options: Unlimited — the sequence number range determines throughput
Exactly 1 — the sender must wait for an ACK before sending the next packet
2 — one per direction (bidirectional)
The window size, which defaults to 4
questions.explanation: Stop-and-wait allows exactly one unacknowledged packet at a time. The sender transmits a packet and halts until the ACK arrives (or it times out and retransmits). This is what makes it 'stop and wait' — it stops after each packet. Pipelining protocols (Go-Back-N, Selective Repeat, TCP) allow a window of N unacknowledged packets, which is what enables high throughput on high-latency links.
